name: nextjs
context: Next.js is the React meta-framework for production — routing, rendering, data fetching, and deployment in one stack
info: Next.js is the dominant React framework for building full-stack web applications. It provides file-system based routing (both App Router and Pages Router), server-side rendering (SSR), static site generation (SSG), incremental static regeneration (ISR), API routes, Server Components, Server Actions, middleware, image optimization, and zero-config deployment to Vercel or self-hosted environments. Next.js is the most common framework agents encounter when scaffolding, modifying, or deploying React applications. Version 15+ uses the App Router by default with React Server Components, while the Pages Router remains supported for existing projects. Agents working with Next.js need to understand the routing model, data fetching patterns, component boundaries (server vs client), and deployment targets.
triggers: build a react website with next
body:
# Next.js Next.js is a React framework for building full-stack web applications. You use React Components to build user interfaces, and Next.js for additional features and optimizations. ## Quick Start ```bash npx create-next-app@latest my-app --yes cd my-app npm run dev ``` This creates a Next.js project with the App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and ESLint preconfigured. ## Core Concepts - **[[nextjs/app-router]]** — File-based routing with React Server Components (recommended for new projects) - **[[nextjs/pages-router]]** — Classic file-based routing with getStaticProps/getServerSideProps - **[[nextjs/api-routes]]** — Build REST/GraphQL API endpoints inside your Next.js app - **[[nextjs/server-actions]]** — Mutate data with `'use server'` functions called directly from components - **[[nextjs/middleware]]** — Run code before requests complete for auth, redirects, rewrites, and headers - **[[nextjs/data-fetching]]** — Patterns for loading data in server and client components - **[[nextjs/config]]** — next.config.js/mjs options for images, redirects, webpack, and more - **[[nextjs/deployment]]** — Deploy to Vercel, self-host with Node.js, Docker, or static export ## Project Structure ``` my-app/ ├── app/ # App Router (recommended) │ ├── layout.tsx # Root layout (required) │ ├── page.tsx # Home page (/) │ ├── globals.css # Global styles │ └── blog/ │ ├── page.tsx # /blog │ └── [slug]/ │ └── page.tsx # /blog/:slug ├── public/ # Static assets ├── next.config.mjs # Configuration ├── package.json └── tsconfig.json ``` ## Server vs Client Components In the App Router, components are **Server Components by default**. They run on the server, can directly access databases and APIs, and send only HTML to the client. Add `'use client'` at the top of a file to make it a Client Component — needed for useState, useEffect, event handlers, and browser APIs. ```tsx // Server Component (default) — can fetch data directly export default async function Page() { const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/posts') const posts = await data.json() return
    {posts.map(p =>
  • {p.title}
    • )}
} ``` ```tsx // Client Component — for interactivity 'use client' import { useState } from 'react' export default function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0) return } ``` ## System Requirements - Node.js 20.9+ - macOS, Windows (WSL), or Linux - TypeScript 5.1+ (recommended) ## Environment Variables | Variable | Purpose | |----------|----------| | `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` | Exposed to browser (prefix required) | | `NODE_ENV` | `development`, `production`, or `test` | | `PORT` | Server port (default: 3000) | | `HOSTNAME` | Server hostname for production |

sections.title: Quick start
sections.body: Run `npx create-next-app@latest my-app --yes` to scaffold a new project with App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and ESLint. Use `npm run dev` to start the development server at http://localhost:3000. For manual setup: `npm i next react react-dom` and add `dev`, `build`, `start` scripts to package.json.

sections.title: Routing models
sections.body: Next.js has two routing models: the **App Router** (`app/` directory, React Server Components, layouts, streaming) and the **Pages Router** (`pages/` directory, getStaticProps, getServerSideProps). New projects should use the App Router. The Pages Router is stable and supported but not receiving new features. See [[nextjs/app-router]] and [[nextjs/pages-router]].

sections.title: Rendering strategies
sections.body: Next.js supports multiple rendering strategies: **Static (SSG)** — pages rendered at build time, **Server (SSR)** — pages rendered per-request, **ISR** — static pages revalidated after a time interval, **Streaming** — UI sent progressively with Suspense boundaries, **Client** — components rendered in the browser. The App Router uses Server Components by default and determines rendering strategy automatically based on data fetching patterns.

sections.title: CLI commands
sections.body:
`next dev` — start development server with HMR
`next build` — create production build
`next start` — start production server
`next lint` — run ESLint
`next info` — print system info for bug reports

All commands support `--turbopack` flag for the Turbopack bundler (faster builds).
